clusters.claim: The audit-plus-statutory-deadline design is acceptable only if the deadline is legally binding and actually enforced; an extendable or toothless deadline is equivalent to indefinite reform deferral dressed up as process.
clusters.interest: The shared interest of the exhausted teaching profession, planning families, the digital-economy/STEM sector, and evidence-first policy actors in ensuring the audit produces a real decision point rather than cover for continued inaction.
clusters.value: Procedural integrity / accountability versus the political convenience of open-ended deferral (credible commitment vs. flexibility).
clusters.fear: That 'reform is coming, maybe' becomes a permanent limbo — the profession keeps absorbing announcements without funding, families cannot plan multi-year gimnazium commitments, and another 1-2 cohorts pass through an unexamined system while nothing is actually decided.
clusters.assumption: That a Hungarian statutory deadline can in practice be made non-extendable and enforced — i.e., that legal binding actually forecloses the path-of-least-resistance deferral rather than being circumvented by escape clauses or at-will extension.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: whether a statutory deadline can actually prevent deferral is untested in Hungary and flagged as an open uncertainty in the scenario itself, so the enforceability question is not factually settled.

clusters.claim: The audit's governance and scope must be genuinely independent — not stacked to pre-determine a case against or for selection, not used to bypass parliamentary or maintainer consultation, and not a backdoor to compel non-state maintainer data-sharing without consent.
clusters.interest: Non-state maintainers (esp. church-maintained schools), conservative value-defenders, and independent researchers share an interest in ensuring the audit is a neutral fact-finding instrument rather than a pre-loaded lever for a predetermined reform outcome or a route to compel maintainer data without consent.
clusters.value: Procedural integrity and institutional autonomy in tension with the reform impulse to use the statutory deadline to force structural change (due process/consent vs. decisive evidence-driven action).
clusters.fear: That the audit's terms of reference, governing body, or deadline mechanism will be engineered to manufacture a case for (or against) selection and to bypass parliamentary and maintainer consultation, stripping maintainers of the negotiated autonomy their agreements protect.
clusters.assumption: That an audit's governance, scope, and deadline can in practice be captured or gamed by whoever designs them, so genuine independence and consent must be structurally guaranteed rather than assumed.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Contested/unknown: whether a legally binding statutory deadline can actually prevent deferral and whether non-state maintainers will cooperate with data requests absent a legal mandate are flagged as untested in Hungary, so the governance design's effectiveness is not settled.

clusters.claim: Commissioning yet another audit is unnecessary institutionalised delay because a diagnosis and reform pathway are already documented, and every month of unchanged sorting under audit is itself a real cost borne by disadvantaged and Roma pupils, not a neutral wait.
clusters.interest: The interest of disadvantaged and Roma pupils (voiced by the education-reform movement and the equal-opportunity civil group) in ending harmful sorting now rather than after another procedural cycle.
clusters.value: Equity and urgency of remedy in tension with procedural caution and evidentiary thoroughness (act-now vs. verify-first).
clusters.fear: That an 18-month audit becomes a legitimising reason to keep an already-diagnosed segregative practice running, deferring relief for the exact children being harmed each additional month.
clusters.assumption: That the existing diagnosis and documented reform pathway are already robust and specific enough to justify acting without a fresh audit, and that delay is a genuine ongoing harm rather than a neutral pause.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The SES/sorting gradient is documented and largely settled, but whether the existing reform pathway is implementation-ready without further audit is contested, so the factual dispute is not about the harm but about readiness to act.

clusters.claim: The audit's terms of reference must explicitly cost the teacher-implementation burden (retraining time, workload, transition) with binding union consultation, or any resulting reform risks being administratively elegant but pedagogically unrealistic and unfunded.
clusters.interest: The interest of frontline teachers and their unions, who bear the concrete labour cost of any reform (retraining time, higher workload, class-size management during transition).
clusters.value: Pedagogical realism and fair treatment of the workforce in tension with administrative/technocratic elegance and speed of reform.
clusters.fear: That reform will again be announced and imposed on an already-exhausted profession as an unfunded expectation, with retraining and transition costs silently downloaded onto teachers.
affected: Students experiencing class-size and continuity effects during transition
The treasury/funder responsible for retraining budgets
clusters.assumption: That an audit designed without an explicit teacher-implementation-cost dimension and binding union consultation will produce reforms that are uncosted and infeasible in practice.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The general fact that unfunded reforms burden teachers is well-established, but whether this specific audit's TOR would omit implementation costing is contested and depends on decisions not yet made.

clusters.claim: An SES-aware, less-coachable admission pilot is defensible only as honest, rigorously monitored evidence-generation on whether the new proxy and assessment actually identify genuine need and aptitude without being gamed, with final criteria published well ahead of the affected application cycle so families and maintainers aren't blindsided.
clusters.interest: A coalition of pilot-district families wanting predictable rules, researchers wanting genuine learning rather than a pre-baked result, the STEM labour sector wanting a preserved aptitude signal, and a durability-minded government wanting a defensible low-commitment step.
clusters.value: Epistemic honesty and procedural fairness (transparent, advance-published, empirically-tested criteria) in tension with political expediency and the pressure to declare a pre-committed reform a success.
clusters.fear: That an admittedly unvalidated proxy and assessment get rolled out on a politically pre-committed timeline, blindsiding compliant families mid-application-cycle and discrediting the whole SES-aware approach.
affected: families and applicants in pilot districts
clusters.assumption: That a gaming-resistant SES proxy, a less-coachable-yet-still-predictive assessment, intensive monitoring, and criteria published a full cycle in advance are all actually achievable within the pilot's compressed timeframe and political constraints.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: whether the new SES proxy resists gaming and whether the redesigned assessment still identifies genuine aptitude (versus merely different demographics) is untested, and the scenario itself flags no existing SES register exists to build the proxy on.

clusters.claim: SES-weighted admission quotas risk displacing genuinely meritorious pupils with an unvalidated, gameable demographic proxy, undermining the meritocratic function of selective tracks; and admission reform alone, without complementary desegregation targets, merely re-labels the queue rather than shortening it.
clusters.interest: Selective-track defenders (conservative value-guardians) protecting high-achieving pupils and non-state maintainer autonomy, alongside equal-opportunity advocates who want intake reform to actually reduce segregation rather than reshuffle it.
clusters.value: Excellence/meritocracy and subsidiarity in tension with equity, plus a sub-tension between procedural admission fairness and substantive desegregation outcomes.
clusters.fear: That an unvalidated, gameable SES proxy edges out genuinely able pupils and coerces autonomous maintainers, while the reform's proponents fear a cosmetic fix that leaves the underlying stratified queue intact.
clusters.assumption: That SES weighting is a poor, manipulable stand-in for disadvantage AND that admission-side changes without binding intake/desegregation targets cannot by themselves shift the composition of who ends up where.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Contested: the validity and gameability of the SES proxy and whether admission reform alone shortens versus re-labels the queue are open empirical questions the scenario itself flags as unvalidated.

clusters.claim: Any admission pilot must not impose new unfunded workload on teachers or override non-state maintainers' admission discretion — teacher resourcing, union participation in design, and maintainer participation must all be voluntary and negotiated, not mandated.
clusters.interest: Teacher unions seeking protection against uncompensated workload increases, and non-state (church/foundation) maintainers seeking to preserve their existing admission discretion over mission-relevant criteria.
clusters.value: Procedural fairness and institutional autonomy in tension with centrally-mandated equity implementation (voluntary/negotiated design vs. imposed reform).
clusters.fear: Teachers fear absorbing unfunded assessment-redesign and heightened compositional heterogeneity as added load, while maintainers fear a decree replacing their mission-based discretion with an externally imposed SES proxy.
clusters.assumption: That a pilot imposed without proportional resourcing or negotiated participation would degrade teaching capacity and provoke maintainer opt-out, and that voluntary, negotiated design is both legally and politically available.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The factual premise is mixed: it is settled that no current legal mechanism compels non-state maintainers to adopt uniform criteria, but the workload/resource impact of SES-driven compositional change is contested and would only be quantified by pilot data.

clusters.claim: Every cohort spared early sorting keeps its weakest members alongside stronger peers, so a phased reduction in selective places produces a genuine peer-effect equity gain, provided the pace of reduction does not outrun support for receiving schools.
clusters.interest: Equity advocates who want the system to protect the weakest students by keeping mixed-ability cohorts intact, so no cohort loses its peer resources through early sorting.
clusters.value: Equity (peer-effect gains for weaker students) in tension with the practical capacity of receiving schools to absorb them without diluting excellence.
clusters.fear: That a well-intentioned reduction in selective places, if rushed, dumps weaker students into schools unprepared to support them, converting a hoped-for equity gain into a shared decline.
affected: stronger peers who remain in mixed cohorts
clusters.assumption: That positive peer effects from keeping weaker students alongside stronger ones are real and net-positive, and that support for receiving schools can realistically be scaled to match any chosen phase-down pace.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Contested: peer-effect evidence exists but its magnitude and direction depend heavily on implementation, and the pace-vs-support threshold is unquantified, making the conditional claim's factual core unsettled.

clusters.claim: Phase-down's equity gain is conditional on preventing non-state maintainers from absorbing displaced demand via regulatory levers that do not yet exist or are legally untested against maintainer autonomy, and it risks overcrowding general schools and forcing teacher redeployment unless compensatory capacity funding is guaranteed and disbursed on schedule.
clusters.interest: It bundles the interests of non-state (church/foundation) maintainers defending their autonomy and enrollment discretion with those of general-school pupils, teachers, and families who bear the overcrowding and redeployment costs if compensatory capacity is not funded on time.
clusters.value: Equity (reducing early sorting) is in tension with maintainer autonomy/subsidiarity and with the child-wellbeing and delivery-feasibility values that guaranteed capacity is meant to protect.
clusters.fear: That the reform will merely relocate stratification into less-regulated non-state tracks while dumping unfunded overcrowding and forced teacher redeployment onto general schools already in a documented shortage.
affected: general-school pupils (especially lower-SES pupils reliant on residual selective places)
the governing majority legislating the schedule
clusters.assumption: That the equity gain depends on legal, enforceable regulatory levers over non-state maintainers existing (or being validatable in advance) and on compensatory capacity funding being ring-fenced and disbursed ahead of each year's place reduction.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: the scenario's own record concedes the constraining regulatory levers do not currently exist and are legally untested against maintainer agreements, and treats non-state absorption of displaced demand as a structural likelihood rather than a settled outcome.

clusters.claim: Hungary's documented systemic teacher shortage (OECD, PSZ/PDSZ data) makes the retraining and capacity buildout that phase-down or abolition require unrealistic on the proposed timeline, risking forced redeployment, larger classes, and accelerated attrition unless binding protections (multi-year phase-in, voluntary redeployment with severance, statutory class-size caps) are legislated first.
clusters.interest: Teachers and their unions (PSZ/PDSZ), plus reform-realist policymakers, seeking to prevent the costs of structural change from being transferred onto an already-overstretched workforce.
clusters.value: Feasibility and worker protection versus reform ambition — implementability and fair burden-sharing in tension with the equity goal driving abolition.
clusters.fear: That abolition on a fixed short timeline forces redeployment, swells class sizes, and accelerates attrition, degrading STEM/digital capacity and collapsing the reform mid-transition without the promised equity gain.
affected: the governing majority bearing the political/fiscal cost
clusters.assumption: That current documented shortage levels cannot be closed by recruitment/retraining within the proposed rollout window, so the timeline itself is the binding constraint unless protections are legislated first.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: Partly settled and partly contested — the shortage is documented (OECD Education at a Glance; PSZ/PDSZ) and the scenario's own implementation_planning concedes a 4-year national capacity build is unlikely, but the exact retraining/redeployment volumes are conceded as unknown, leaving the achievable phase-in pace genuinely uncertain.

clusters.claim: Abolishing the 6/8-year track is the only scenario that removes the sorting mechanism itself rather than adjusting its dials, matching a documented reform programme's call for unified general education as long as possible.
clusters.interest: Equity-oriented civil actors and an education-reform movement that want to eliminate early social sorting rather than merely soften its effects.
clusters.value: Equity and social integration (unified general education for all) in tension with excellence/differentiation and parental choice for gifted or ambitious children.
clusters.fear: That any reform short of removing the 6/8-year track leaves the structural sorting machine intact, so early selection keeps reproducing elite social separation.
affected: students entering at age 10-12 (especially lower-SES families)
existing 6/8-year gimnázium students, staff and their families
teachers who would need retraining and support systems
middle-class families using early-selection tracks as a mobility strategy
clusters.assumption: That the sorting mechanism is the track structure itself, so only structural abolition (not dial-adjustment) can end the sorting, and that the documented reform programme's diagnosis transfers to this decision.
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: The causal claim that early tracking drives social separation is well-supported in the literature, but whether abolition alone removes sorting (rather than displacing it into residential/informal channels) is contested and depends on the bundled support systems.

clusters.claim: The equity case for abolition rests on a cross-national association between early tracking and SES gradients rather than Hungary-specific causal evidence, and the scenario's own uncertainty record concedes sorting could simply migrate into school choice, catchment, or non-state tracks — so the reform's full cost could be paid without its promised equity gain.

clusters.fear: the benefit claimed will not materialise as described

clusters.claim: Abolition overrides parental choice, maintainer autonomy (including church/foundation institutions' 6/8-year formational mission), and an established talent-development pathway, and risks disrupting currently young children's school community and commute unless catchment rules and continuity safeguards are locked in before implementation.
clusters.interest: the concern(s) raised by konzervativ_ertekvedo, egyhazi_fenntartoi, kisgyerekes_szuloi
clusters.value: a trade-off between the scenario's stated goal and the concern raised
clusters.fear: the benefit claimed will not materialise as described

clusters.claim: Structural reform risks being reversed once political consensus shifts, as happened in Poland (2016-2019), wasting its equity gains and the government's political capital unless entrenched by a legally binding mechanism stronger than an informal multi-party pact.
clusters.interest: the concern(s) raised by kormanyzati_reformrealizmus, fuggetlen_szakpolitikai_kutatomuhely
clusters.value: a trade-off between the scenario's stated goal and the concern raised
clusters.fear: the benefit claimed will not materialise as described
clusters.assumption: the claim's premise holds under implementation as designed
clusters.empirical_uncertainty: not independently graded here — see the evidence layer

clusters.claim: Targeted support for general schools (curriculum standards, TEIP-style funding, reduced grade retention) is the only scenario that improves conditions for the majority of pupils without disrupting existing choice, structure, or maintainer arrangements — but it delivers real gains only if the funding is made durable and ring-fenced, since the scenario's own cost analysis flags it as vulnerable to fiscal-consolidation cuts.
clusters.interest: the concern(s) raised by pedagogus_erdekvedo, kisgyerekes_szuloi, konzervativ_ertekvedo, digitalisgazdasagi, egyhazi_fenntartoi, kormanyzati_reformrealizmus, fuggetlen_szakpolitikai_kutatomuhely, pedagogus_szakszervezeti_hang
clusters.value: a trade-off between the scenario's stated goal and the concern raised
clusters.fear: the benefit claimed will not materialise as described

clusters.claim: Compensating general schools without structural change risks legitimising and permanently entrenching the selective structure's segregating effect rather than fixing it, so it is acceptable only as a time-bounded interim measure with published segregation-reduction targets and a stated structural trajectory, not as a substitute for eventual structural reform.
clusters.interest: the concern(s) raised by eselyegyenlosegi_civil, oktataspolitikai_reformmozgalom, fuggetlen_szakpolitikai_kutatomuhely
